name: Jiaxin (Jerry) Qian
title: Senior Backend Engineer
summary: Senior Backend Engineer with 8+ years of experience designing and delivering scalable, reliable financial services infrastructure in regulated environments. Deep expertise in Java/Spring Boot microservices, event-driven architectures (Kafka), and REST API design. Proven track record in high-integrity, compliance-critical systems processing millions of transactions daily, with strong emphasis on observability, fault tolerance, and long-term maintainability.

experience.title: Senior Backend Engineer (Contract)
experience.context: Regulatory filings platform: ~15-25 microservices, 50k-100k transactions/day, Kafka event-driven workflows, PostgreSQL persistence
bullets: Designed and delivered Java Spring Boot backend services powering high-volume regulatory filing and transaction processing in a heavily regulated environment, processing millions of financial records daily with strict consistency, auditability, and zero-loss guarantees.
Architected idempotent, retry-safe REST APIs and distributed transaction workflows ensuring correct state management and financial data integrity across 15-25 microservices.

Built Kafka event-driven pipelines for real-time financial data ingestion and processing, with robust retry logic, dead-letter handling, and failure-domain isolation guaranteeing reliable downstream consumption.
Reduced API latency by 30% through systematic SQL query optimisation and backend service refactoring, delivering measurable performance improvements for high-frequency financial data workloads.
Led legacy system modernisation efforts, refactoring aging service components into clean, testable Spring Boot microservices while maintaining backward compatibility.

Established comprehensive observability infrastructure (Splunk/Prometheus): structured logging, monitoring dashboards, alerting, and failure analysis enabling proactive reliability management and fast production incident resolution.
Drove CI/CD pipeline improvements (Jenkins/Git) and championed engineering best practices; conducted rigorous code reviews and mentored engineers on distributed systems design and testing.
Collaborated closely with product owners and engineering leadership to translate regulatory and business requirements into scalable, production-ready backend solutions.

experience.title: Backend Engineer
bullets: Built Java Spring Boot microservices and REST APIs supporting transactional business workflows and secure cross-system integrations across internal platforms.
Optimised SQL queries and relational database interactions, improving throughput for data-intensive operational processes.
Automated deployment pipelines, reducing manual release effort by ~60% and improving deployment consistency and rollback reliability.
Mentored engineers on testing practices, CI/CD standards, and backend code quality.

experience.title: Java Software Engineer
bullets: Developed high-throughput transactional backend APIs (Java + RabbitMQ) handling concurrent reservation processing.
Implemented distributed validation and consistency logic preventing duplicate transactions and data corruption under high concurrency.

experience.title: Java Backend Developer
bullets: Built enterprise Java/Spring MVC backend services for regulated financial (insurance) policy management platforms.
Grew automated test coverage from 15% to 75% (JUnit), establishing quality culture and significantly reducing production defects.
Performed SQL and backend performance tuning across legacy services, improving throughput for high-frequency financial data operations.

kafka_pipeline.why_kafka_over_rest: The workflow had multiple long-running stages: validation, enrichment, persistence, notifications. With synchronous REST, a mid-flow failure required manual recovery. Kafka gave us durability and replay — each stage modeled as an event-driven state transition, reprocessable from offset. Also absorbed traffic spikes during bulk filing periods instead of overwhelming downstream services.
kafka_pipeline.failure_handling: Two-layer approach: local retries with exponential backoff for transient issues (DB locks, network). If retries exhausted, message routed to DLQ with error reason and original payload. Built an internal replay tool to filter DLQ messages by error type and re-publish to original topic. All consumers designed idempotent so replay was always safe.
kafka_pipeline.hardest_bug: Schema drift: producer added a new field, consumer used older deserialization model. No crash — fields silently defaulted, corrupting downstream data. Only caught through data reconciliation, not alerts. Fix: added strict schema validation at consumer boundary and enforced schema versioning to prevent silent field drops.

latency_improvement.discovery: User complaints about slow filings triggered investigation. Checked Grafana — p95/p99 trending up, not yet alerting but degradation was clear.
latency_improvement.root_cause: Two issues found via request tracing: (1) N+1 query pattern fetching reference data inside a loop, (2) high-frequency query missing a composite index, causing full table scans.
latency_improvement.fix_and_validation: Replaced N+1 with batch query. Added composite index aligned with filter conditions. Validated using p95/p99 metrics and EXPLAIN plans to confirm index usage. Gradual rollout. Result: ~30% latency reduction with significantly more stable tail latency.

idempotent_api.key_design: Client-provided requestId as idempotency key, server-generated fallback. Stored in PostgreSQL — either dedicated idempotency table or unique constraint on business table — within the same transaction for strong consistency.
idempotent_api.duplicate_detection: On request: check if key exists. If yes, return stored result. If no, insert key and process in same DB transaction. Prevents race conditions at the DB level.
idempotent_api.failure_case: Early implementation only enforced idempotency at API layer, not Kafka consumers. During a consumer rebalance, same message processed twice — duplicate writes. Fix: standardized idempotency across both APIs and consumers using DB-level uniqueness constraints. Retries and reprocessing guaranteed single successful write.
